items.question: State the SVD A = U Σ Vᵀ and explain what σᵢ, uᵢ and vᵢ represent geometrically.
items.answer: U and V are orthogonal bases; σᵢ ≥ 0 scale input direction vᵢ to output direction uᵢ. Geometrically, A rotates to singular-vector coordinates (Vᵀ), scales axis i by σᵢ, then rotates to output coordinates (U).

items.question: How do singular values relate to eigenvalues of AᵀA?
items.answer: Non-zero singular values σᵢ satisfy σᵢ² = λᵢ(AᵀA); columns of V are eigenvectors of AᵀA. Left singular vectors are eigenvectors of AAᵀ with the same non-zero eigenvalues.

items.question: Why is truncated SVD used for image compression, and what controls the tradeoff?
items.answer: Keep top k singular values for best rank-k approximation (Eckart–Young). Larger k preserves more detail but costs more storage; smaller k compresses more but blurs fine structure. Error ||A - A_k||_F equals sqrt(sum of discarded σᵢ²).

items.question: When would you use randomized SVD instead of full `np.linalg.svd`?
items.answer: For very large dense or sparse matrices when only top-k singular values/vectors are needed. Randomized methods approximate the dominant spectrum with far less time and memory than full O(min(mn², m²n)) SVD.
